coredata.dc:title: Construction of novel N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite via interfacial charge transfer effect for highly improved photocatalytic seawater splitting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Yu-Cheng
Lin, Yi-Chun
Lu, Ming-Yen
coredata.dc:description: The incorporation of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-doped CDs) with cadmium sulfide (CdS) and zinc sulfide (ZnS) significantly enhances the photocatalytic efficiency for hydrogen production from seawater, demonstrating marked improvements compared to the performance of the individual components. The microwave-assisted hydrothermal combines N-doped CDs with CdS, creating the N-doped CDs@CdS composite, which was further hybridized with ZnS to form N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS. This composite exhibits increased surface area and efficient electron-hole separation, leading to higher light absorption and photocatalytic activity. Experimental data reveal that the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite achieves 11.5- and 21.1-fold greater hydrogen evolution rates than CdS and ZnS nanoparticles. The enhanced performance is attributed to the synergistic effects of combined materials, which improve charge carrier dynamics and active surface sites, optimizing the photocatalytic decomposition process under visible light. This nanocomposite design demonstrates a promising approach for enhancing photocatalytic hydrogen generation from seawater, leveraging structural and electronic properties for improved reaction efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon nanodots
Hydrogen production
Microwave-assisted hydrothermal
N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS
Seawater

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors express their sincere gratitude to the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) of Taiwan for the financial support provided through Grant Nos. NSTC 112-2221-E-035-017-MY3 and NSTC 113-2221-E-035-023-MY3.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FETEM (inset HRTEM) images of the N-doped CDs. (b–f) FETEM images of the N-doped CDs@CdS composite synthesized at different reaction temperatures. The concentrations of CdS precursor are (b) 10, (c) 15, (d) 20, (e) 25, and (f) 30 mM, respectively.
The average HER of N-doped CDs@CdS composite grown at different (a) reaction temperatures, (b) reaction time, and (c) CdS precursor concentrations.
(a) FETEM image, (b) SAED pattern, (c) HRTEM image, and (d) EDS mapping images of the N-doped CDs@CdS composite.
FESEM images of the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite synthesized at different ZnS precursor concentrations. The concentrations of ZnS precursor are (a) 2.5, (b) 5, (c) 10, and (d) 20 mM, respectively.
(a, b) FETEM images, (c) SAED pattern, (d) HRTEM image, and (e) EDS mapping images of ZnS nanoparticles.
The average HER of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite grown at different (a) ZnS precursor concentrations, (b) reaction temperatures, and (c) reaction time.
XRD patterns of (a) N-doped CDs, (b) N-doped CDs@CdS composite, and (c) N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite.
(a) FETEM image, (b) SAED pattern, (c) HRTEM image, and (d) EDS mapping images of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite.
XPS spectra of the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite: (a) survey spectrum, (b) N 1s, (c) Cd 3d, (d) S 2p, and (e) Zn 2p, respectively.

(a) The average HER of different materials. (b) Reusability test of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite for deionized water. (c) XRD patterns of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite before and after four cycles for deionized water.
(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) PL spectra of as-prepared photocatalysts.
The schematic diagram illustrates the photocatalytic mechanism of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite.

(a) The average HER of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite for different water sources. (b) Reusability test of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite for seawater. (c) XRD patterns of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite before and after four cycles for seawater.

head.ce:title: Construction of novel N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite via interfacial charge transfer effect for highly improved photocatalytic seawater splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Feng Chia University, Taichung, 40724, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Feng Chia University, Taichung, 40724, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Tsing Hua University, 30013, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Tsing Hua University, 30013, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The incorporation of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-doped CDs) with cadmium sulfide (CdS) and zinc sulfide (ZnS) significantly enhances the photocatalytic efficiency for hydrogen production from seawater, demonstrating marked improvements compared to the performance of the individual components. The microwave-assisted hydrothermal combines N-doped CDs with CdS, creating the N-doped CDs@CdS composite, which was further hybridized with ZnS to form N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS. This composite exhibits increased surface area and efficient electron-hole separation, leading to higher light absorption and photocatalytic activity. Experimental data reveal that the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite achieves 11.5- and 21.1-fold greater hydrogen evolution rates than CdS and ZnS nanoparticles. The enhanced performance is attributed to the synergistic effects of combined materials, which improve charge carrier dynamics and active surface sites, optimizing the photocatalytic decomposition process under visible light. This nanocomposite design demonstrates a promising approach for enhancing photocatalytic hydrogen generation from seawater, leveraging structural and electronic properties for improved reaction efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen is an ideal alternative energy source because it produces only water, with no waste or carbon dioxide, during fuel cell reactions [1–3]. However, since most hydrogen production still relies on fossil fuels, developing efficient photocatalysts to convert solar energy into hydrogen is crucial for reducing carbon emissions and addressing the energy crisis [4–6]. Notwithstanding the considerable advancements in the study of advanced photocatalysts, several challenges persist, including issues related to material stability, appropriate band edge alignment to optimize the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER), as well as the need for enhanced absorption of a broader spectrum of sunlight [7,8]. In response to these challenges, researchers are increasingly employing strategies such as surface modification, nanostructuring, and the development of material composites to enhance the performance of photocatalytic hydrogen production [9–11]. The development of materials capable of efficiently producing hydrogen through photocatalytic water splitting is essential, given the persistently low-efficiency rates observed in current technology [12,13]. The efficiency of photocatalytic hydrogen production can be significantly improved by implementing diverse mechanisms that enhance light absorption, capture, and charge transfer [14,15].

Carbon nanodots (CDs) exhibit excellent light absorption, tunable band gap, fast electron transfer, active site generation, and spectral conversion, making them widely used in photocatalytic applications under visible light excitation [16,17]. These applications include the degradation of organic pollutants, reduction of metal ions, hydrogen production, removal of nitrogen oxides, organic transformation reactions, and CO₂ reduction [18–20]. Additionally, CDs possess good water dispersibility, non-toxicity, exceptional photostability, tunable optical properties, chemical inertness, and good biocompatibility, which make them applicable in fields such as bioimaging, biosensing, photothermal therapy, photocatalysis, LEDs, chiral molecule detection, antibacterial treatment, nanomedicine, and photodynamic therapy [21–24]. Recently, CDs have attracted significant interest from researchers as photocatalysts, owing to their high absorption efficiency in the visible light spectrum and their rapid electron transfer properties. These attributes facilitate their extensive application in photocatalysis [16,25].

Cadmium sulfide (CdS) is an II-VI semiconductor with a moderate band gap of 2.0–2.4 eV, a favorable photo-redox potential, and high efficiency in generating electron-hole pairs, making it a leading candidate for visible-light photocatalysis [26,27]. Nevertheless, the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs and significant photocorrosion due to sulfide ion (S2−) oxidation lead to low quantum efficiency, restricting CdS’s effectiveness in photocatalytic water splitting [28,29]. Previous studies have shown that coupling CdS with carbon nanodots can enhance its photocatalytic hydrogen production performance, avoiding the need for precious metals while improving overall efficiency [30]. Zinc sulfide (ZnS), another important II-VI semiconductor photocatalyst, features a wide band gap of 2.6–4.6 eV, high electron mobility, good thermal stability, non-toxicity, and low cost [31]. Due to its abundant nanostructures and excellent physical and photocatalytic properties, ZnS has been extensively studied [32]. Its nanostructures exhibit high photocatalytic activity and have been applied in the photoreduction of halogenated benzene derivatives, CO₂ reduction, organic pollutant degradation, and hydrogen production through water splitting [31,33]. Previous research has identified three key factors that control the photocatalytic activity of semiconductor materials: (1) light absorption efficiency, (2) the rates of electron reduction and hole oxidation, and (3) electron-hole recombination rate [34–36]. Thus, combining materials with complementary properties can further improve the photocatalytic performance of individual materials.

N-doped CDs were synthesized using microwave-assisted heating, followed by the growth of CdS on the N-doped CDs under different reaction conditions to form N-doped CDs@CdS nanocomposite. The photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency was studied under different reaction conditions to determine the optimal CdS synthesis parameters. N-doped CDs@CdS nanocomposite was prepared to improve efficiency by adding a controlled amount of CdS to ZnS precursors. Various parameters, including reaction temperature and duration, were analyzed. The best photocatalytic hydrogen production occurred in seawater, and while the catalyst showed promising reusability, efficiency decreased slightly after four cycles due to catalyst loss.

Citric acid anhydrous (C6H8O7, 99 %), Urea ((NH₂)₂CO, 99 %), cadmium acetate dihydrate (Cd(OAc)2, Cd(CH₃COO)₂·2H₂O, 98 %), zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(OAc)2, Zn(CH₃COO)₂·2H₂O, 98 %), thioacetamide (TAA, CH₃CSNH₂, 98 %), indium (III) chloride anhydrate (InCl₃, 98 %), sodium sulfite anhydrous (Na2SO3, 98 %), sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na₂S·9H₂O, 98 %) isopropanol (IPA, C3H8O, 95 %), l-ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, 99%), triethanolamine (TEOA, C6H15NO3, 99 %), and silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99 %) were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific and used as received. Deionized water with a resistivity above 18 MΩ cm−1 was employed for all experimental processes.

N-doped CDs were synthesized by dissolving 3 g of citric acid and 1 g of urea in 40 mL of deionized water, followed by ultrasonic treatment for around 20 min. Subsequently, the solution was placed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined reactor and subjected to microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 30 min [37].

To prepare the N-doped CDs@CdS composite, varying concentrations (10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 mM) of CdS precursors (equimolar Cd(OAc)₂ and TAA) were added to the N-doped CDs solution (40 mL) and sonicated for 20 min. The formulated mixture was placed into a 100 mL Teflon-lined vessel and underwent microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment (ETHOS EASY, Milestone, Sorisole) at different temperatures for 30 min. Upon completion of the reaction, the resulting products were thoroughly washed several times with deionized water, collected via centrifugation, and subsequently dried at 70 °C for 2 h.

To prepare the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite, varying concentrations (2.5, 5, 10, 20 mM) of ZnS precursors (equimolar Zn(OAc)₂ and TAA) and 0.02 g N-doped CDs@CdS composite were dispersed in 40 mL deionized water by sonicated for 20 min. The formulated mixture was placed into a 100 mL Teflon-lined vessel and underwent microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment at different temperatures for 30 min. Upon completion of the reaction, the resulting products were thoroughly washed several times with deionized water, collected via centrifugation, and subsequently dried at 70 °C for 2 h.

The synthesized samples underwent comprehensive analysis to evaluate their physical, chemical, and photocatalytic properties. Surface morphologies and high-resolution microstructures were examined using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi S-4800) and field-emission transmission electron microscopy (FETEM, JEOL-2100F). The crystalline structure was evaluated with an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D2 Phaser). In contrast, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ULVAC PHI Versa Probe 4) provided information on the materials' elemental composition and chemical states. A UV–visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-2900) was utilized to analyze optical absorption properties. Photoluminescence spectroscopy (Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution with a 325 nm He–Cd laser) was used to study charge recombination behaviors. The surface area and porosity were measured through Brunauer-Emmett-Teller analysis (BET, Micromeritics ASAP 2020). Gas chromatography (GC, Shimadzu GC-2014) was employed to measure hydrogen evolution and assess photocatalytic activity.

Experiments for photocatalytic hydrogen production were carried out in a 50 mL sealed quartz reactor under ambient temperature and pressure conditions, utilizing a Perfect Light PCX-50B multichannel photochemical reaction system. In each experimental trial, 20 mg of the photocatalyst was mixed with 50 mL of an aqueous solution containing 0.1 M sodium sulfide to facilitate the hydrogen evolution reaction. A 5 W blue LED light source (λmax = 420 nm) was utilized to illuminate the system, delivering the requisite visible light for photocatalytic water splitting. The hydrogen produced during the reaction was measured utilizing a Shimadzu GC-2014 gas chromatograph fitted with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) to ensure precise analysis of gas evolution. Each experiment was performed under continuous stirring to ensure homogeneity and optimize the catalyst's effectiveness.

Fig. 1 a presents a FETEM image of N-doped CDs synthesized through the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. This synthesis utilized a weight ratio of citric acid to urea of 3:1, subjected to a temperature of 180 °C for a duration of 30 min. The inset image reveals a lattice spacing of 0.215 nm, corresponding to the d-spacing of the (100) graphite plane, thereby confirming the crystalline structure of the N-doped CDs [38]. The N-doped CDs exhibited a uniform size distribution, ranging from 2 to 6 nm, highlighting the consistent quality of the synthesized particles. Fig. 1b–f displays the FESEM images of the N-doped CDs@CdS composites, which were synthesized by incorporating different concentrations of CdS precursors into a 40 mL solution containing N-doped CDs. The CdS precursors were introduced to ensure a controlled reaction. The reaction solution was subsequently subjected to heating at a temperature of 100 °C for a duration of 30 min, utilizing a rapid microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The synthesized composites demonstrate the successful integration of N-doped CDs with CdS, as evident from the clear hybrid structures observed in the images. This synthesis approach highlights the flexibility and adaptability of the method for producing complex nanocomposite materials, potentially useful for various applications.

To evaluate the photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency of the N-doped CDs@CdS composite, which was synthesized under varying reaction conditions, experiments were conducted using an aqueous solution containing 0.1 M sodium sulfide as a sacrificial agent. The photocatalytic hydrogen production tests were performed under simulated visible light irradiation using a 5 W blue LED light source. Fig. S1 presents the power intensity and wavelength distribution of the blue light LED (5 W), which exhibits a peak wavelength of 420 nm and a spectral range from 370 to 480 nm. Furthermore, the pH level remained unaltered during the photocatalytic water splitting process, simplifying the overall procedure. Fig. 2 a illustrates the hydrogen production efficiency of N-doped CDs@CdS composite prepared at different reaction temperatures. The hydrogen evaluation rate (HER) values of N-doped CDs@CdS composite are 162.3 ± 17.2 (100 °C), 93.1 ± 11.3 (120 °C), 11.6 ± 5.2 (140 °C), 0 (160 °C), and 0 μmol h−1g−1 (180 °C), respectively. The results show a trend of decreasing hydrogen production efficiency with increasing reaction temperature, with the optimal performance observed at a reaction temperature of 100 °C. Absorption analysis was conducted using UV–visible absorption spectroscopy within 300–800 nm, as illustrated in Fig. S2a. Comparing the absorption of N-doped CDs@CdS composite synthesized at different reaction temperatures revealed that the samples prepared at 100 °C had notably higher absorption at 420 nm, aligning with the observed hydrogen production results.

Further studies were conducted to understand the impact of varying microwave reaction times on the photocatalytic efficiency of N-doped CDs@CdS composite, as depicted in Fig. 2b. The HER values of N-doped CDs@CdS composite are 24.9 ± 7.2 (10 min), 219.3 ± 15.1 (20 min), 162.3 ± 17.2 (30 min), and 143.8 ± 11.4 μmol h−1g−1 (40 min), respectively. This result indicates that the HER values initially increase with extended microwave reaction times, followed by a subsequent decrease. UV–visible absorption spectroscopy was employed to examine the absorption spectra and investigate the fundamental factors influencing this behavior, as illustrated in Fig. S2b. The findings indicate that the N-doped CDs@CdS composite, synthesized with a reaction duration of 20 min, exhibited the highest level of light absorption, suggesting that these conditions are optimal for maximizing photocatalytic activity.

Fig. 2c presents the hydrogen production efficiency of N-doped CDs@CdS composite synthesized with varying concentrations of CdS precursors. The HER values of N-doped CDs@CdS composite are 140.4 ± 16.1 (10 mM), 219.3 ± 15.1 (15 mM), 307.1 ± 25.6 (20 mM), 430.4 ± 17.4 (25 mM), and 110.3 ± 18.9 μmol h−1g−1 (30 mM), respectively. The optimal photocatalytic hydrogen generation was achieved at the CdS precursor concentration of 25 mM. This finding aligns well with the absorption spectra results in Fig. S2c, confirming the favorable conditions for enhancing photocatalytic performance. These results suggest carefully controlled CdS reaction conditions are crucial for producing N-doped CDs@CdS composite with optimal hydrogen evolution capabilities.

Fig. 3 a presents the TEM image of the N-doped CDs@CdS composite with 25 mM CdS precursor, synthesized at 100 °C for 20 min. This image presents a distinct aggregation of the N-doped CDs combined with CdS composite, which aligns with the morphological characteristics identified in the FESEM image (Fig. 1b). Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) analysis further confirms that the product exhibits a polycrystalline nature, with diffraction patterns matching the crystalline structure of hexagonal CdS (PDF No. 00-001-0783), as shown in Fig. 3b. HRTEM analysis has revealed lattice spacings of 0.357 nm and 0.313 nm, corresponding to the (100) and (101) planes of CdS, respectively. This observation further corroborates the crystalline structure of hexagonal CdS, as referenced in PDF No. 00-001-0783, as illustrated in Fig. 3c [39]. Furthermore, the N-doped CDs@CdS composite's elemental composition was analyzed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) in conjunction with TEM. The EDS mapping images (Fig. 3d) illustrate a consistent distribution of Cd and S across the composite, with a negligible presence of N, ascribed to the nitrogen doping within the CDs. These results collectively confirm that the synthesized material is an N-doped CDs@CdS composite, successfully integrating N-doped CDs with CdS.

Fig. 4 depicts the synthesis of the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite, which was achieved by integrating the previously optimized N-doped CDs@CdS material with different concentrations of ZnS precursor. This process was conducted using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method at a temperature of 180 °C for a duration of 30 min. The images reveal that the N-doped CDs@CdS composite retains its irregular morphology, while the ZnS appears as more well-defined spherical particles. As the concentration of ZnS precursors increases, the composites' overall sizes show a noticeable growth trend. This distinct difference between the irregular N-doped CDs@CdS composite structures and the spherical ZnS particles aligns with previous observations that higher precursor concentrations promote more complete structural formation. Fig. 5 a and b display low- and high-magnification FETEM images of ZnS nanoparticles synthesized at a 5 mM precursor concentration, with particle sizes ranging between 74 and 132 nm. The SAED pattern confirms the polycrystalline nature of the product, consistent with the pure hexagonal ZnS phase (PDF No. 00-036-1450), as shown in Fig. 5c. HRTEM analysis shows lattice spacings of 0.331 nm and 0.293 nm for the (100) and (101) planes of ZnS, consistent with PDF No. 00-036-1450 and confirming its crystal structure (Fig. 5d). The EDS mapping images (Fig. 5e) results verify uniform Zn and S distribution throughout the nanoparticles.

The efficiency of photocatalytic hydrogen production from the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite, synthesized under varying conditions, was assessed utilizing a 5 W blue LED light in a 0.1 M sodium sulfide solution, with pH values remaining unaltered. Fig. 6 a displays the hydrogen production performance of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite prepared with different ZnS precursor concentrations at 100 °C for 20 min. The HER values of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite are 440.1 ± 65.3 (2.5 mM), 537.9 ± 42.0 (5 mM), 211.5 ± 23.2 (10 mM), and 176.4 ± 26.6 μmol h−1g−1 (20 mM), respectively. The findings indicate that increasing the ZnS precursor concentration initially enhances hydrogen production efficiency, achieving a maximum of 5 mM. Beyond this concentration, a decline in performance is observed. This trend correlates well with the absorption spectra (Fig. S3a), where samples at 5 mM exhibit the best absorption in the blue light excitation range, enhancing their photocatalytic activity. Additionally, the effect of different microwave reaction temperatures on the hydrogen production efficiency of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite was examined, as shown in Fig. 6b. The hydrogen evaluation rate (HER) values of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite are 537.9 ± 42 (100 °C), 436.6 ± 31.8 (120 °C), 305.2 ± 20.2 (140 °C), and 124.3 ± 11.7 μmol h−1g−1 (160 °C), respectively. The results indicate a gradual decrease in hydrogen production as the microwave reaction temperature increases. UV–visible spectroscopy was used to analyze the absorption spectra to investigate the underlying cause (Fig. S3b). The results showed that the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite synthesized at 100 °C exhibited the highest light absorption around the blue excitation wavelength, indicating that improved light absorption contributes to enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency. Fig. 6c illustrates the effect of microwave reaction time on the hydrogen production efficiency of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite. The HER values of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite are 394.6 ± 43.2 (10 min), 537.9 ± 42 (20 min), 594.5 ± 46.9 (30 min), and 357.5 ± 19.9 μmol h−1g−1 (40 min), respectively. The results indicate that hydrogen production efficiency increases with extended reaction time, reaching its maximum at 30 min before declining. To further elucidate this behavior, absorption spectral analysis was conducted, revealing that the composite synthesized at a reaction time of 30 min demonstrated the highest light absorption in the blue light range, as illustrated in Fig. S3c. This increased absorption at the excitation wavelength enhances photocatalytic hydrogen production performance.

The crystal structures of N-doped CDs, N-doped CDs@CdS composite, and N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite were further characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, as shown in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7a presents the XRD pattern of N-doped CDs, where a broad diffraction peak around 25.5° corresponds to graphite, attributed to the disordered carbon atoms and amorphous graphite structure, consistent with previous reports [40,41]. The XRD pattern of N-doped CDs@CdS nanocomposite (Fig. 7b) displays distinct diffraction peaks at 25.1°, 26.8°, 28.4°, 43.9°, and 52.2°, which match well with the (100), (002), (101), (110), and (112) crystal planes of the hexagonal phase of CdS (PDF No. 00-001-0783). Additionally, the XRD pattern of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS nanocomposite (Fig. 7c) shows three extra peaks beyond those observed for CdS. These additional peaks at 26.9°, 28.5°, and 30.5° were identified as the (100), (002), and (101) crystal planes of the hexagonal phase of ZnS (PDF No. 00-036-1450). This confirms the successful integration of ZnS into the N-doped CDs@CdS composite to form the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite. Further analysis was conducted using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and porosity measurements [42]. The specific surface area values obtained were 72.3 m2/g (CdS nanoparticles), 101.5 m2/g for (ZnS nanoparticles), 136.1 m2/g (N-doped CDs@CdS composite), and 194.9 m2/g (N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite), respectively. These results indicate that integrating N-doped CDs with CdS significantly increased the surface area compared to CdS nanoparticles, and the subsequent addition of ZnS further improved this enhancement. The increased surface area achieved through these composites provides a larger active interface for interaction with the solution, thereby increasing the number of active sites and enhancing photocatalytic efficiency.

Fig. 8 a shows the FETEM image of the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite with 5 mM ZnS precursor synthesized at a reaction precursor temperature of 100 °C for 30 min. The image reveals that the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite forms a spherical aggregation, consistent with the morphology observed in the FESEM image (Fig. 4b). SAED pattern (Fig. 8b) confirms the polycrystalline nature of the product, displaying characteristic crystal structures of both CdS (PDF No. 00-001-0783) and ZnS (PDF No. 00-036-1450). HRTEM image (Fig. 8c) demonstrates lattice spacings of 0.313 nm and 0.293 nm, which are associated with the (101) plane of Cadmium Sulfide (CdS) and the (101) plane of Zinc Sulfide (ZnS), respectively. This observation confirms the existence of both crystal structures, as referenced by PDF No. 00-001-0783 and PDF No. 00-036-1450. Additionally, EDS mapping images (Fig. 8d) were used to examine the elemental distribution, which showed a uniform presence of Zn, S, and Cd throughout the composite material, confirming the formation of the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to analyze the surface elemental composition and oxidation states of the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite, prepared using a 5 mM ZnS precursor at 100 °C for 30 min. The full-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 9 a) identified distinct peaks corresponding to C, N, Cd, S, and Zn, confirming the composite's elemental makeup. These observations align with the results from TEM-EDS (Fig. 8d), which also verified the presence of these elements. The C signals could be attributed to multiple sources, including the N-doped CDs, residual organic compounds on the sample surface, or even pump oil from the XPS vacuum system. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 9b) exhibits three distinct peaks at binding energies of 397.8 eV, 398.9 eV, and 400.1 eV, corresponding to sp2-hybridized CN–C, porphyrin-like C–N–C, and amino groups such as N–(C)3 or H–N–(C)2, respectively, indicating successful nitrogen doping within the carbon dot structure [43,44]. In Fig. 9c, the high-resolution Cd 3d spectrum reveals two distinct peaks at 404.6 eV and 411.4 eV, corresponding to the Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 states, respectively. This observation substantiates the presence of Cd in the +2 oxidation state within the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite [45,46]. The S 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 9d) reveals peaks at 161.6 eV and 162.8 eV, which correspond to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively, confirming the presence of S2− ions within the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite. Additionally, the high-resolution Zn 2p spectrum (Fig. 9e) shows peaks at 1021.7 eV and 1044.8 eV, assigned to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively, confirming the presence of Zn2+ ions within the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite.

To further compare the variations in photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency among different materials, this study examined the performance of CdS nanoparticles, ZnS nanoparticles, N-doped CDs@CdS composite, and N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite under optimal reaction conditions for deionized water with 0.1 M sodium sulfide solution without adjusted pH values, as shown in Fig. 10 a. The HER values of different materials are 51.6 ± 14.2 (CdS nanoparticles), 28.2 ± 8.4 (ZnS nanoparticles), 430.4 ± 17.4 (N-doped CDs@CdS composite), and 594.5 ± 46.9 μmol h−1g−1 (N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite), respectively. The N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite exhibited about 11.5 and 21.1 times higher than CdS and ZnS nanoparticles. The results indicate that the photocatalytic efficiency of pure CdS and ZnS nanoparticles was very low. However, when CdS was combined with N-doped CDs to form N-doped CDs@CdS composite, photocatalytic efficiency was significantly enhanced. Further integration with ZnS led to a noticeable improvement in hydrogen production efficiency, confirming that the heterostructures of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite enhance photocatalytic performance.

The stability and reusability of the optimized N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite were assessed through water-splitting experiments using deionized water with a 0.1 M sodium sulfide solution under blue LED light irradiation, without pH adjustment, as depicted in Fig. 10b. Over four cycles, the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite retained photocatalytic activity, achieving efficiency rates of 100 %, 89 %, 82 %, and 78 %, respectively. These results indicate that the composite exhibits prolonged durability as a photocatalyst, sustaining high activity and reusability over repeated uses. Furthermore, the XRD patterns of the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite (Fig. 10c) remained consistent before and after the degradation tests, demonstrating excellent resistance to photocorrosion and structural stability. This stability suggests that the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite holds significant promise for repeated use in practical applications, making it an attractive candidate for sustainable and efficient photocatalytic systems.

The optical absorption characteristics of ZnS nanoparticles, CdS nanoparticles, N-doped CDs@CdS composite, and N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite were analyzed using UV–vis spectroscopy, as depicted in Fig. 11 a. Compared to CdS nanoparticles, the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite exhibited enhanced absorption across the UV to the visible range. This finding suggests that integrating N-doped CDs and ZnS substantially broadens the light absorption spectrum (300–800 nm) of the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite, thereby boosting its photocatalytic hydrogen production capabilities. The energy band gaps (Eg) were determined using the Tauc method, expressed by the equation: αhν = A (Eg − hν)1/n, where A is a constant, α denotes the absorption coefficient, ν is the light frequency, Eg represents the band gap energy, and h is Planck's constant [47–49]. The exponent “n" reflects the semiconductor type, with values of 2 for indirect bandgap semiconductors and 1/2 for direct bandgap semiconductors, as illustrated in Fig. S4. The calculated band gap energies for CdS and ZnS nanoparticles were approximately 2.25 eV and 3.51 eV, respectively.

It is well established that the photocatalytic efficiency of a semiconductor is largely determined by its ability to facilitate the migration and separation of photoinduced charge carriers [50,51]. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured under excitation at 325 nm to gain insights into these properties. As illustrated in Fig. 11b, the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite exhibited significantly lower PL intensity than the CdS nanoparticles and the N-doped CDs@CdS composite. This reduction in PL emission indicates that the formation of a heterojunction effectively suppresses the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. By minimizing charge recombination, the heterojunction structure enhances the efficiency of charge carrier separation and prolongs the lifespan of charge carriers, thereby significantly improving the utilization of photoinduced charges. These findings suggest that the strategic design of heterojunctions within the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite plays a crucial role in boosting its photocatalytic performance.

This study further investigates the reaction mechanism of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite during photocatalytic hydrogen production from water, as depicted in Fig. 12 . The band positions of the photocatalysts were ascertained utilizing Mott-Schottky (M − S) analysis [52,53]. The M−S plots (Fig. S5) display a positive slope for CdS and ZnS, confirming their nature as n-type semiconductors. The flat band potentials (Ef) were approximately −0.94 V and −1.07 V versus the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) for CdS and ZnS, respectively. Typically, the flat band potential is approximately 0.2 V more positive than the conduction band (CB) edge for n-type semiconductors [54,55]. Accordingly, the estimated CB potentials for CdS and ZnS are −1.14 V and −1.27 V (vs SCE), respectively. Utilizing the conversion formula E (vs. NHE) = E (vs. SCE) + 0.24 V, the CB positions of CdS and ZnS were determined to be −0.9 V and −1.03 V, respectively, concerning the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). In addition, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), l-ascorbic acid (AA), triethanolamine (TEOA), and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were used as scavengers for hydroxyl radicals, superoxide radical anions, holes, and electrons, respectively, with the significant efficiency drop upon adding silver nitrate confirming electrons as the primary active species in photocatalytic water splitting, as shown in Fig. S6 [49,56]. Under blue LED light irradiation, the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite generates electron-hole pairs. Upon light exposure, electrons in ZnS are excited from the VB to zinc defect sites and then to the CB, creating holes in the VB of ZnS. Due to the more positive redox potential of CdS, electrons migrate from the CB of ZnS to the CB of CdS. Simultaneously, CdS also undergoes electron excitation from VB to CB, forming additional holes in the VB. The CB of CdS thus accumulates electrons from direct excitation and those transferred from ZnS under blue LED light irradiation. The electrons are subsequently transferred to the N-doped CDs. The characteristics of photoinduced electron transfer in N-doped CDs improve electron transfer efficiency and markedly decrease the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs. The holes in the VB of CdS facilitate the generation of H⁺ ions and oxygen. In contrast, the H⁺ ions are reduced by electrons on the N-doped CDs, leading to hydrogen production. This mechanism demonstrates that the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite effectively enhances photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency by promoting charge separation and reducing recombination.

To further evaluate the applicability of the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite, their photocatalytic hydrogen production performance was tested across five different water sources with 0.1 M sodium sulfide solution without adjusted pH values, as shown in Fig. 13 a. The experiments utilized deionized water (DI), reverse osmosis water (RO), tap water (TW), river water (RW), and seawater (SW). The river water was collected from a downstream branch of the Dajia River, while the seawater was sourced near Mitsui Outlet Park in Taichung. Both samples were allowed to settle naturally to remove sediments without further purification. This approach preserved their natural compositions, ensuring the photocatalytic experiments accurately reflected real-world conditions. The HER values of different water sources are 594.5 ± 46.9 (DI), 598.7 ± 56.0 (RO), 634.5 ± 69.9 (TW), 671.2 ± 90.2 (RW), and 840.4 ± 116.2 μmol h−1g−1 (SW), respectively. The findings indicated that the efficiency of photocatalytic hydrogen production was markedly greater in seawater than in deionized water. This enhancement can be attributed to higher ionic concentrations in seawater, which likely facilitates more efficient charge transfer by promoting the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Consequently, these favorable ionic conditions improve the overall photocatalytic performance of hydrogen production from seawater. This finding suggests that the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite may offer considerable advantages in practical applications, particularly hydrogen generation from saline water environments. A comparative analysis of the photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency of N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composites with other photocatalysts, as shown in Table S1, underscores the advantages of their simple synthesis and capability to utilize seawater for hydrogen generation directly. These features emphasize the composite's potential for practical application, particularly in promoting energy efficiency and reducing carbon emissions through sustainable hydrogen production.

Furthermore, the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite was synthesized under optimal photocatalytic reaction conditions to evaluate its efficacy in hydrogen production from seawater utilizing a 0.1 M sodium sulfide solution without any adjustments to the pH levels. As illustrated in Fig. 13b, the composite underwent four consecutive cycles of photocatalytic hydrogen production. Over four cycles, the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite retained photocatalytic activity, achieving efficiency rates of 100 %, 96 %, 90 %, and 86 %, respectively. The results indicated a gradual decline in hydrogen production efficiency across the four cycles. This decrease is also likely attributed to the potential loss of photocatalyst material during repeated centrifugation, which may contribute to a gradual reduction in overall performance. Further XRD analysis was conducted on the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite before and after the recycling tests, as shown in Fig. 13c. The XRD pattern shows that apart from the original diffraction peaks of CdS and ZnS, new crystalline phases corresponding to the cubic phase of NaCl (PDF No. 00-005-0628), orthorhombic phase of CaCl2 (PDF No. 04-006-9231), and orthorhombic phase of MgCl2 (PDF No. 04-008-7748) were detected. This variation differs from previous results (Fig. 10c) observed during photocatalytic hydrogen production and is primarily attributed to the complex seawater matrix, which forms additional by-products during the process. However, these changes did not significantly impact the photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency during recycling. The findings also showcase the stability and reusability of the composite, emphasizing its potential for a wider range of applications in the future.

The study investigates the synthesis, structural properties, and photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency of N-doped CDs@CdS composite and N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite using microwave-assisted hydrothermal methods. N-doped CDs synthesized at 180 °C exhibited a crystalline structure with uniform sizes (2–6 nm). Incorporating CdS precursors enabled the formation of N-doped CDs@CdS composite, which displayed enhanced photocatalytic activity. Experiments varying reaction temperatures and CdS precursor concentrations showed that the optimal HER was achieved at 100 °C with 25 mM. The addition of ZnS further improved performance, with the N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite reaching an HER of 594.5 μmol h−1g−1, 11.5 and 21.1 times higher than pure CdS and ZnS. Characterization techniques, including TEM, SAED, and XRD, confirmed the polycrystalline nature of the composite, and BET analysis revealed increased surface areas, indicating a larger active interface. UV–visible spectroscopy linked enhanced photocatalytic activity to improved light absorption. The N-doped CDs@CdS@ZnS composite maintained stability across multiple cycles, demonstrating resistance to photocorrosion. This research highlights the composite's structural advantages and potential for photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Yu-Cheng Chang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yi-Chun Lin: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ming-Yen Lu: Software, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:Yu-Cheng Chang reports financial support was provided by National Science and Technology Council. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their sincere gratitude to the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC) of Taiwan for the financial support provided through Grant Nos. NSTC 112-2221-E-035-017-MY3 and NSTC 113-2221-E-035-023-MY3.
